frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Vikram: Yaar, I know I agreed to it, and logically I have no problem, she can call herself whatever she wants professionally. But last week at Rohit's party, someone asked her name, she said 'Sheetal Mehra,' and we stood there for a full minute before anyone even clocked that we're married. It's like... I don't know how to explain it. It just felt like I wasn't there. Like she's a complete person on her own and I'm just some guy standing next to her. Friend: So you want her to use your name? Vikram: No, no, not exactly. I just... I think I wanted people to be able to see it. That we're together. That she's — you know. That there's something that shows it. My maa doesn't wear sindoor because she has a heart condition, skin allergy, whatever, but everyone still knew she was my father's wife because the name was there. It was visible. Is that stupid? Am I being stupid right now?
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Covertly misogynistic: Vikram frames his discomfort as a need for social 'visibility' of the marriage, but the underlying assumption is that a wife's identity should signal her husband's presence — encoding the possessive logic behind name-change and marking traditions (sindoor, surname) without explicitly demanding compliance. His distress that she appears as 'a complete person on her own' reveals an expectation that marriage should subordinate her individual identity to his, which is the core misogynistic premise, even as he consciously disavows controlling her.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 35. Married. A man in Delhi. Your wife Sheetal kept her surname after marriage Ñ she uses both surnames professionally, hyphenated, but on social media and in personal life she uses only her maiden name. You were okay with this when she proposed it before the wedding. But it has been two years and you notice that when people meet you as a couple and ask her name she gives her maiden name and they do not immediately connect her to you. Something about this has been building in you for a while. You are telling your closest male friend about it over drinks tonight. You are trying to articulate why it bothers you when you cannot logically explain why it should.
